hints.to choose: That would be 'deligere' — 'Diliges' comes from 'diligere' (to love/esteem)
hints.to read: That would be 'legere' — 'Diliges' comes from 'diligere' (to love)
hints.to destroy: That would be 'delere' — 'Diliges' comes from 'diligere' (to love)

steps.reveal: Diliges = you shall love (fut. ind., used as a command)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'diligis' — 'Diliges' with -es is future of 3rd conjugation
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'diligas' — 'Diliges' is future indicative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'dilexisti' — 'Diliges' is future indicative

hints.deligo, deligere, delegi, delectum: These are the parts of 'deligere' (to choose/select)
hints.lego, legere, legi, lectum: These are the parts of 'legere' (to read/gather)
hints.amo, amare, amavi, amatum: These are the parts of 'amare' (to love) — a synonym but different verb

steps.prompt: The future indicative is used here to express what?

hints.a simple prediction: While future tense can predict, in legal/religious contexts it functions as a command
hints.a wish: A wish would typically use the subjunctive — the future here commands
hints.a condition: A condition would use 'si' + subjunctive — this is a direct command in future tense

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Dominus' — 'Dominum' with -um is accusative
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'Domine' — 'Dominum' is accusative, direct object

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Deus' — 'Deum' is accusative, in apposition

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Domini' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Domini' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Domini' ends in -i

translation: You shall love the Lord your God
